responses.strategic_rationale: The project's core tension lies in balancing rigorous physiological data capture with operational sustainability, participant safety, and ethical constraints. Key trade-offs involve resource allocation between high-fidelity monitoring and scalability, privacy protections versus data utility, and methodological innovation versus institutional conservatism. Critical levers must address these interdependencies while navigating the physical and regulatory constraints of a residential research model.

levers.name: Staffing Coverage Model
levers.consequences: Adjusting shift patterns affects real-time event detection and technician fatigue. A 12-hour shift model reduces coverage gaps but risks burnout, while a 8-hour rotating schedule increases staffing costs but maintains alertness. Cross-training non-clinical personnel could lower expenses but may compromise safety protocol adherence.
options: Implement a 12-hour shift model with mandatory 30-minute rest blocks between monitoring cycles
Establish a rotating 8-hour shift schedule with staggered start times to maintain 24/7 coverage
Cross-train research assistants to assist with non-clinical monitoring tasks during peak event windows
levers.review_lever: Balancing coverage duration vs. staff endurance; underestimates cumulative cognitive load from repeated event response protocols

levers.name: Sensor Fusion Strategy
levers.consequences: Prioritizing certain sensor types influences event detection accuracy. Focusing on EEG headbands increases portability but reduces spatial resolution compared to fixed PSG setups. Integrating environmental sensors enhances contextual data but requires complex synchronization workflows.
options: Deploy hybrid dry-electrode EEG headbands with embedded inertial sensors for movement tracking
Install fixed-array EEG electrodes during enhanced-night PSG sessions with real-time artifact detection
Integrate pressure-sensitive mattress arrays with acoustic anomaly detection for multi-modal event correlation
levers.review_lever: Optimizing sensor specificity vs. complexity; overlooks potential interoperability challenges between proprietary data formats

levers.name: Participant Retention Mechanism
levers.consequences: Compensation structures impact enrollment quality and compliance. Increasing hourly rates may attract less motivated participants while reducing overall sample diversity. Offering academic credit could improve recruitment from student populations but introduces selection bias.
options: Implement a tiered compensation structure linking payment milestones to event capture frequency
Offer research credit toward clinical training requirements for medical students and residents
Create a loyalty program with incremental rewards for participants completing extended stays
levers.review_lever: Incentivizing event yield vs. sample representativeness; fails to address potential coercion concerns in extended stay agreements

levers.name: Data Annotation Workflow
levers.consequences: Centralizing annotation increases consistency but creates bottleneck risks. Decentralizing across multiple raters improves throughput but reduces inter-rater reliability. Automating initial triage accelerates processing but requires ongoing algorithm validation.
options: Establish a dual-rater system with mandatory consensus meetings for ambiguous events
Develop a cloud-based annotation platform with version control and audit trails for remote scoring
Implement machine learning pre-screening with human-in-the-loop validation for edge cases
levers.review_lever: Efficiency vs. reliability in scoring processes; underestimates the time required for algorithm retraining cycles

levers.name: Facility Expansion Protocol
levers.consequences: Accelerating expansion risks compromising safety standards while delaying expansion may limit data collection capacity. Modular renovations maintain flexibility but require ongoing infrastructure investments. Repurposing existing spaces preserves budget but may introduce design constraints.
options: Adopt a phased expansion approach with temporary safety upgrades for new suites
Convert adjacent residential units into auxiliary monitoring spaces with standardized safety checks
Develop a mobile monitoring trailer for overflow capacity during peak enrollment periods
levers.review_lever: Growth trajectory vs. operational stability; ignores potential regulatory hurdles in repurposing non-traditional spaces

levers.name: Control Group Design
levers.consequences: Matching controls to participants improves statistical validity but restricts recruitment flexibility. Using general population controls increases sample diversity but reduces comparability. Implementing active control measures enhances data quality but raises ethical concerns.
options: Recruit control participants through workplace wellness programs with similar sleep schedules
Implement a double-blind control protocol with placebo sleep interventions for half the group
Use historical control data from previous sleep studies with compatible inclusion criteria
levers.review_lever: Control group relevance vs. ethical considerations; overlooks potential confounding variables in historical data

levers.name: Regulatory Compliance Framework
levers.consequences: Proactive engagement with ethics boards accelerates approvals but requires ongoing communication resources. Reactive compliance reduces administrative burden but increases review delays. Standardizing documentation across institutions improves efficiency but creates template rigidity.
options: Establish a dedicated regulatory affairs coordinator for continuous ethics board engagement
Develop a modular compliance documentation system adaptable to multiple institutional review boards
Implement a pre-submission feedback loop with ethics committee members during protocol design
levers.review_lever: Proactive oversight vs. resource allocation; underestimates variability in regional regulatory requirements

responses.strategic_rationale: The project's core tension lies in balancing rigorous physiological data capture with participant safety, operational sustainability, and ethical compliance. Key trade-offs involve resource allocation between sensor fidelity and participant comfort, expansion timelines versus data quality assurance, and control group validity against resource constraints. Critical levers must address these interdependencies while navigating regulatory and logistical complexities in a physical research environment.

levers.name: Participant Engagement Protocol
levers.consequences: Enhancing incentives increases retention but raises compensation costs. Community partnerships may diversify recruitment but risks diluting clinical specificity. Peer mentorship programs could improve adherence but require additional training resources and may introduce bias in self-reported data.
options: Implement tiered incentive structures based on participation duration
Establish community partnerships to co-host educational workshops about parasomnia research
Introduce peer mentorship programs where long-term participants guide new enrollees through the study process
levers.review_lever: Balancing engagement metrics against budget constraints risks overinvesting in retention at the expense of data quality. The options overlook potential conflicts between mentorship dynamics and objective data collection.

levers.name: Sensor Calibration Schedule
levers.consequences: Daily calibration maintains precision but consumes technician time. Third-party audits improve objectivity but add external dependencies. Automated self-checks reduce labor but may miss subtle drifts in sensor accuracy during extended deployments.
options: Conduct pre-night calibration using standardized reference signals for all sensor types
Partner with independent technical auditors for biweekly sensor performance validation
Deploy machine learning models to detect and correct sensor drift in real time
levers.review_lever: Prioritizing calibration frequency versus technician workload creates a resource trade-off. The options fail to address potential conflicts between automated corrections and human oversight in critical event detection.

levers.name: Safety Protocol Review
levers.consequences: Adding redundant safety checks reduces incident risk but increases operational complexity. Staff training improves response times but requires dedicated time away from data collection. Community feedback loops enhance contextual awareness but may introduce subjective biases into safety assessments.
options: Integrate biometric fall detection systems with existing alarm protocols
Rotate safety drills across all staff roles including night technicians and data analysts
Create a resident safety advisory panel with former participants and local emergency responders
levers.review_lever: Ensuring safety while maintaining naturalistic behavior creates a paradox. The options miss potential conflicts between surveillance measures and participant comfort in a domestic setting.

levers.name: Control Group Matching Criteria
levers.consequences: Stricter criteria improve statistical validity but limit enrollment speed. Biomarker-based matching enhances precision but requires additional testing resources. Dynamic adjustment protocols adapt to enrollment trends but complicate data analysis workflows.
options: Use actigraphy data to select controls with comparable movement patterns during sleep
Implement a dual-screening process combining clinical interviews and sleep diary reviews
Adopt a rolling matching algorithm that adjusts criteria based on real-time enrollment metrics
levers.review_lever: Balancing control group representativeness against recruitment efficiency creates a bottleneck. The options neglect potential disparities in data quality between tightly matched controls and more diverse cohorts.

levers.name: Data Synchronization Protocol
levers.consequences: Real-time synchronization enables immediate anomaly detection but increases network demands. Periodic batch processing reduces infrastructure costs but delays event response. Centralized hub architectures improve coordination but create single points of failure.
options: Establish time-stamped metadata headers for all sensor streams during initial data acquisition
Implement a decentralized synchronization framework using edge computing nodes in each sleep suite
Create cross-validated timestamping protocols between EEG-BIDS files and raw sensor logs
levers.review_lever: Maintaining temporal precision across heterogeneous data sources creates technical complexity. The options understate risks of synchronization errors during multi-modal event capture.

levers.name: Facility Maintenance Schedule
levers.consequences: Preventive maintenance reduces breakdowns but consumes non-research hours. Third-party contracts ensure expertise but create dependency risks. Staff training improves responsiveness but requires dedicated education time.
options: Schedule quarterly deep-cleanings during low-enrollment periods to minimize disruption
Develop in-house maintenance protocols using modular sensor components for easy replacement
Create a cross-training program where technicians rotate through maintenance and data collection roles
levers.review_lever: Balancing facility upkeep against research operations creates scheduling conflicts. The options fail to address potential skill gaps in non-technical staff performing maintenance tasks.

levers.name: Alternative Recruitment Channels
levers.consequences: Academic collaborations expand reach but may prioritize research-focused participants. Local partnerships increase accessibility but risk selection bias. Online outreach broadens demographics but requires careful privacy safeguards.
options: Leverage sleep clinic patient registries for targeted outreach to individuals with documented parasomnia histories
Partner with regional occupational health services to identify at-risk populations
Develop a community ambassador program with local wellness centers and fitness facilities
levers.review_lever: Expanding recruitment sources risks diluting clinical specificity. The options overlook potential conflicts between outreach strategies and the facility's controlled environment requirements.

responses.strategic_rationale: The project's core tensions revolve around balancing clinical rigor with participant comfort, optimizing resource allocation under budget constraints, and ensuring ethical compliance without stifling operational agility. Critical levers must address physical environment design, community integration, and adaptive governance to navigate these conflicts while maintaining methodological integrity and scalability.

levers.name: Facility Layout Optimization
levers.consequences: Reconfiguring suite layouts to prioritize modular adaptability enhances scalability but risks compromising the domestic atmosphere that encourages naturalistic behavior. A centralized sensor hub reduces wiring complexity but may create focal points for participant anxiety.
options: Implementing reconfigurable furniture systems for dual-purpose sleep and observation zones
Integrating ambient light therapy panels to simulate diurnal cycles and reduce arousal thresholds
Deploying ceiling-mounted sensor arrays to eliminate floor clutter and preserve spatial openness
levers.review_lever: Balancing modularity vs. comfort; misses long-term impact on participant habituation patterns

levers.name: Community Engagement Strategy
levers.consequences: Establishing local partnerships improves recruitment efficiency but creates dependency on external referral networks. Public workshops increase transparency but may attract non-target participants seeking clinical care.
options: Creating a neighborhood advisory board with local physicians and residents for co-design input
Developing multilingual informational kiosks in regional healthcare facilities
Hosting annual sleep health symposia with open-access research findings
levers.review_lever: Aligning outreach vs. exclusivity; overlooks potential dilution of specialized participant pool

levers.name: Participant Screening Protocol
levers.consequences: Expanding inclusion criteria increases sample diversity but raises risk of comorbidities interfering with parasomnia detection. Peer review panels improve consistency but add administrative overhead.
options: Implementing standardized sleep diary analysis as preliminary screening tool
Creating a dual-tier referral system with primary care physicians and sleep specialists
Using actigraphy data from 30-day pre-screening periods to identify high-yield candidates
levers.review_lever: Inclusivity vs. specificity; fails to address variability in self-reported symptom accuracy

levers.name: Local Healthcare Collaboration
levers.consequences: Shared training programs enhance staff competence but create intellectual property sharing risks. Co-located clinics improve emergency response times but may blur research vs. clinical boundaries.
options: Establishing joint simulation drills with University Hospital Bonn's emergency services
Creating a shared digital platform for real-time patient monitoring data
Developing cross-disciplinary certification programs for sleep technicians
levers.review_lever: Resource sharing vs. autonomy; neglects potential conflicts in data ownership protocols

levers.name: Ethical Review Process
levers.consequences: Continuous oversight maintains compliance but slows protocol adjustments. Participant feedback loops improve transparency but may introduce bias in data interpretation.
options: Implementing quarterly ethics impact assessments with community representatives
Creating a dynamic consent framework with adjustable data usage parameters
Establishing a whistleblower protection program for research staff
levers.review_lever: Compliance vs. adaptability; underestimates challenges of real-time ethical decision-making

levers.name: Sensor Placement Strategy
levers.consequences: Ceiling-mounted arrays reduce visual intrusion but limit anatomical coverage. Floor-based pressure sensors improve event detection but compromise participant privacy.
options: Using magnetic resonance-compatible sensor clusters for multi-modal data fusion
Deploying flexible textile-based sensors embedded in bedding materials
Creating a tiered sensor network with high-density zones around critical pathways
levers.review_lever: Data fidelity vs. participant comfort; overlooks long-term sensor reliability under continuous use

levers.name: Emergency Response Protocol
levers.consequences: On-site medical staff improve intervention speed but increase staffing costs. Pre-arranged hospital partnerships reduce overhead but create transfer delays. Automated alert systems enhance coverage but risk false positives.
options: Implementing a dual-response system with on-site technicians and rapid-response ambulance contracts
Creating a wearable emergency beacon system with biometric triggers
Establishing a 24/7 telemedicine consultation link with University Hospital Bonn
levers.review_lever: Safety vs. cost efficiency; misses potential for technology-induced complacency

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season adds a fixed cost that erases the per-unit savings unless utilization reaches year-round levels."
     - "Each additional clinical site requires its own IRB approval, site-initiation visit, and staff credentialing — a sequential overhead that compounds rather than parallelizes, so doubling site count does not halve enrollment time."
     - "Pooling catastrophe risk across three coastal regions reduces expected annual loss on paper, but a single regional hurricane season can correlate all three simultaneously, turning the diversification assumption into a concentration risk at the worst possible moment."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Length Limits**
   - Keep each `review_lever` under 2 sentences (aim for 20–40 words). Name the tension and the missed weakness concisely.
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
